channel.title: Ctfs on SuperTsumu
channel.description: Recent content in Ctfs on SuperTsumu

item.title: Battle of 1337 2022 preselection writeups
item.description: Home Battle of 1337 2022 preselection writeups BO1337 Website This local CTF was surely an experience for me. I managed to get my first “first blood” since I started participating in CTFs. Huge thanks to the sponsors for funding the event and the staff for creating these awesome challenges. Semerah Padi - 100 [Networking] Streamline - 176 [Networking] Break The Storage - 50 [Web] Rayquaza - 50 [Misc] Darchrow - 100 [Misc]

item.title: ICTF_2022/Grep
item.description: Home Grep - 60 points - Misc Description Grab That Easy Flag Script Kiddie! Creator: Mohin Paramasivam The Writeup Figure 1: Quote by Ken Thompson In this challenge, we are given the file named “Readme”, which you can download here. According to my expert analysis, “Readme” is a file containing ±14 Kilobytes of ASCII text data. To read the raw data off the terminal, I used the unix command “cat” to print the data in the file:

item.title: ICTF_2022
item.description: Home ICTF 2022 Writeups June 11th, 2022. The 2022 APU Internal CTF was hosted for the students of Asia Pacific University of Technology and Innovation. Being a student there, I had to take on their challenges! This was actually my first on-site ctf; I got to meet a lot of great people and have a pleasant time with them. Here are of the writeups for 2 of the challenges I solved during my time there.

item.title: ICTF_2022/The_Meetup
item.description: Home The Meetup - 150 points - OSINT Description My agent just sent me this image as the location for our next secret meetup. Where exactly is this? (This question does not use the BAT22{} flag format. Answer with the exact street or building name this image is taken from.) Creator: Nicholas Mun The Writeup In this challenge, we are given the image file named The_Meetup.jpg. You can download this file here.

item.title: VishwaCTF/The_Library
item.description: Home The Library - 449 points - OSINT, Cryptography Description: Send a ‘hello’ to “The Librarian” from the bot list on the Discord server, and he shall guide you further. The Library is a 5 level OSINT challenge with hints of ancient/classical cryptography. We are pointed to the discord bot in the Vishwa CTF discord server named “The Librarian”: Level 1 After sending a “hello” to the librarian, we get:

item.title: VU_Cyberthon/When_universes_collide
item.description: Home When universes collide - 50 points - Forensics, Cryptography We are given this file Challenge 1.png. Instinctively we would all scan this qr code. We get this string in return: otp!áth:,+totp/No~d%20AccÏ%nt:vuctf2>2Ò@b¥s{paâa$iÚeîcÏm?algïrn|hc=SHA1&digiws=6.issuå}=No}d+AcCounz&.e~ißd=30%sek"åt=6ZZKUEJDOVFYC4OIQBU2PLPYBH54VXWB We spent some time thinking what this could mean. But the qr code and its resulting string was actually bait. To actually get the flag we placed the qr code image in stegsolve. After going through a couple of planes we found this:

item.title: 1Gram
item.description: Home 1Gram - 100 points - OSINT On the same site given in Back to the future, clicking on any of the profile boxes will give you a username for an instagram profile: We can find part of the flag in the posts: Looking at the posts individually we can see tags to other accounts with similar posts showing other parts of the flag. Piecing together the letters, we can get the flag.

item.title: Back To The Future
item.description: Home Back To The Future - 50 points - OSINT We were given a website for this challenge. Giving the challenge’s hints to the past, I looked for past screenshots of the site in The Wayback Machine and found a screenshot from the 4th of July 2022. Viewing the screenshot gives us the flag:

item.title: Break The Storage
item.description: Home Break The Storage - 50 points - Web We get sent to a website with a username and password input field: Trying to log in with incorrect credentials will trigger this pop-up: Upon inspecting the source code of index.js?v=1337, We can see that upon successful login, we will be redirected to profile.html. Going straight to profile.html will redirect you back to login.html if the correct credentials are not entered. I used Burp Suite’s proxy to intercept and stop the site from redirecting me to login.

item.title: CyberGrabs_0x03
item.description: Home CyberGrabs 0x03 writeups Mr. Robot - 200 [Forensics]

item.title: Darchrow
item.description: Home Darchrow - 100 points - Misc We get the following image from this challenge: Instead of using OCR software to get the text out of the image, I meticulously typed the letters for the sake of accuracy: ACZQQOKLHUALHPTXGXKEPWTLDJUCEORHRKTQRTVKXOWRBYACBUKRUPCEA QRAKMZRJHCEHZJWJKSGTLTMOXTEJHLPPEHXJBQQWKXYQNKFTOKBJUVFRUF CSQMTXHTTDJSOFWHYNSOHVEZGQVURJFJALJEXQWWYWIZBJLMOYDMGNXOMR MLQRSYWZHJGBLCSHNMYFXESJNFBBITRXHKZYQGYIPEUYTNFXSSPCXIZJMR CTLHUUHBFEXIVBMEURYMPAZATXUVNVQRSLPFVWWBBUHOEMXYRPMLTYZXLH SAPMMMQOEHXKQCDWBSWDMTFMSMFBNCQGMQHHJPQYKJPZNMYYDKZYZXUHOO HAIAFGMDBMYAEQPRSUVQKGEZSA Looking up “Darchrow consumer of worlds” on the web, we can find a Dota 2 character named Darchrow, the Enigma. I immediately placed the ciphertext into dcode.

item.title: Mr._Robot
item.description: Home Mr. Robot - 200 points - Forensics Description: Mr. Robot most famous TV show but least people know about it. Flag Format: CYBERGRABS{} Author: Tech Wizard This forensics challenge comes with the file chall.wav. Listening to the file and reading the title, this seems to be a monologue from the American tv show Mr Robot. Solution: I googled “mr robot wav files” which pointed me to this site which writes about a tool called DeepSound that was used in the tv series.

item.title: picoCTF_2022
item.description: Home picoCTF 2022 writeups picoCTF 2022 picoCTF is an annual high school ctf hosted by the prestigious Carnegie Mellon University. As usual, they came up with challenging and engaging challenges this year that made us utilize our practical skills. What I love about picoCTF’s challenges is that they make you use skills that you would use on-the-field which makes it a great place to learn practical skills. I alongside Team Excelsior tried our best for #816/7794 globally.

item.title: Rayquaza
item.description: Home Rayquaza - 50 points - Misc In this challenge, we were given the file “1337Pikachu”. Using the Linux “file” command tells us that we are dealing with a Game Boy Advance ROM / GBA file. After spending about an hour trying different Game Boy Advance emulators, I got to boot the ROM using VisualBoyAdvance after giving the file a .gba file extension: And after a few minutes of gameplay, I managed to get the flag from the fat guy as stated in the challenge description:

item.title: Redpoint
item.description: Home Redpoint - 443 points - Misc This image is given for the challenge: After using every forensics tool I know on the image, I have come to the conclusion that the flag was the screwdriver. Flag: BO1337{screwdriver}

item.title: Semerah Padi
item.description: Home Semerah Padi - 100 points - Networking We open the flaghere.pcap file in Wireshark to analyze the packets. We can find some http packets with Lorem ipsum as the content. We can view the whole conversation by following the tcp stream: Hidden within the Lorem Ipsum, we can find a suspicious string that resembles a scrambled web address: After some time trying different transposition ciphers, using the rail fence cipher, we can get a valid url.

item.title: Snap
item.description: Home Streamline - 100 points - OSINT There weren’t many hints from this challenge other than it suggesting that the challenge remains on Instagram. Since the previous challenge is Instagram related, I took a look back at the challenge profiles and noticed that some of the profile images look very strange. Using Toolzu’s Instadp, I was able to download the profile pictures from the previous challenge. Piecing them together gives us this full image:

item.title: Streamline
item.description: Home Streamline - 176 points - Networking We are given an image file, this time named LamboRidzuan: Using stegseek, I am able to bruteforce a hidden file using rockyou.txt. I renamed it to its original file name of handshake.cap and opened it with wireshark. Looking at the protocol hierarchy of the packets, we do not have a lot to look at: This is because the data has been encrypted. We can search for the handshake by inserting “eapol” into the filter bar:

item.title: Torrent Analyze
item.description: Home Torrent Analyze - 400 points - Forensics Description: SOS, someone is torrenting on our network. One of your colleagues has been using torrent to download some files on the company’s network. Can you identify the file(s) that were downloaded? The file name will be the flag, like picoCTF{filename}. Hint: The file in question ends with .iso We are given a pcap file. Open the file on Wireshark to get started!

item.title: Vishwa CTF 2022
item.description: Vishwa CTF 2022 is an international ctf based in India. I alongside my teammates at Team Excelsior scored 103rd out of the 748 teams that played. I really enjoyed the challenging OSINT and Forensics challenges that makes the player scavenge the edges of the web for flags. Home Vishwa CTF 2022 writeups The Library [OSINT]

item.title: VU_Cyberthon_2022
item.description: VU Cyberthon is an international ctf based in Lithuania made by the guys in the Faculty of Informatics of Vytautas Magnus University. I alongside my teammates at Team Excelsior scored 28th place out of the 400+ teams that played against us. It was an interesting experience despite it being very challenging. Thanks to VU Cyberthon for the great time. Home VU Cyberthon 2022 writeups When universes collide [Forensics]
